title: Trade Settlement Failure Monitoring
spl:
index=trading (sourcetype="settlement:status" OR sourcetype="fix:exec")
| search settlement_status IN ("FAIL","PARTIAL") OR ExecType IN ("8","9")
| stats count, sum(quantity*price) as notional by symbol, contra_broker, trade_date
| sort -notional
description: Failed or partial settlements create counterparty and liquidity risk; early detection supports regulatory reporting.
value: Failed or partial settlements create counterparty and liquidity risk; early detection supports regulatory reporting.
implementation: Correlate FIX ExecType with back-office settlement files; alert on fails above threshold or concentrated by broker.
visualization: Table (failed trades), Bar chart (fails by symbol), Timeline (settlement chain).
knownFalsePositives: Settlement fails during counterparty or market holidays, test environments feeding prod-like volumes, or late Swift repair queues; use ops calendars.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: OMS/EMS, clearing TA.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `sourcetype="fix:exec"`, `sourcetype="settlement:status"`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Correlate FIX ExecType with back-office settlement files; alert on fails above threshold or concentrated by broker.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=trading (sourcetype="settlement:status" OR sourcetype="fix:exec")
| search settlement_status IN ("FAIL","PARTIAL") OR ExecType IN ("8","9")
| stats count, sum(quantity*price) as notional by symbol, contra_broker, trade_date
| sort -notional
```

Understanding this SPL

**Trade Settlement Failure Monitoring** — Failed or partial settlements create counterparty and liquidity risk; early detection supports regulatory reporting.

Documented **Data sources**: `sourcetype="fix:exec"`, `sourcetype="settlement:status"`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): OMS/EMS, clearing TA. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: trading; **sourcetype**: settlement:status, fix:exec. Those sourcetypes align with what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=trading, sourcetype="settlement:status". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Applies an explicit `search` filter to narrow the current result set.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by symbol, contra_broker, trade_date** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Step 3 — Validate
Run the search on recent production data, spot-check a handful of results against the source system or operational runbooks (cash operations, EHR, payment gateway, etc.), and adjust thresholds to match your seasonal patterns and test windows so teams are not flooded with noise.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (failed trades), Bar chart (fails by symbol), Timeline (settlement chain).
grandmaExplanation: In Financial Services, we pay close attention to "Trade Settlement Failure Monitoring" in our day-to-day work so that fraud, waste, and compliance issues do not slip by quietly. We help teams focus on the few situations that need a person, not every routine blip in the data.
dataModelAcceleration: Not applicable for the primary search: it relies on application- and industry-specific log formats, not a normalized CIM data model. Optional mapping to CIM, if you adopt it, is a separate follow-on project.
